coredata.dc:title: Numerical optimization of the cooling effect of the bionic spider-web channel cold plate on a pouch lithium-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jianfeng
Liu, Xiaodong
Liu, Fen
Liu, Yiqun
Wang, Fuqiang
Yang, Na
coredata.dc:description: For lithium-ion battery thermal management system for electric vehicles, the insertion of metal plates with channels on both sides of a lithium-ion battery is an effective method for a high-temperature cooling. Inspired by the nature spider-web structure, we propose a cold plate with a bionic spider-web channel inside. Based on the heat generation characteristics of the pouch lithium-ion battery at an extreme discharge rate of 12 C, we used an orthogonal experimental design to study the cooling performance of the spider web-channel structure for a lithium-ion battery. Numerical simulation results showed that, among the structural parameters of the spider-web channel, the channel width had the largest influence on the cooling performance of the cold plate, while the channel angle had the smallest influence. The increase in the channel width could not indefinitely enhance the cooling performance of the cold plate. The most suitable structure was obtained at a width of 3 mm. When the channel angle was 120°, the pouch lithium-ion battery could provide the best thermal balance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Thermal management
Cold plate
Bionic spider-web structure
Orthogonal experimental design

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Heat generation model of the pouch lithium-ion battery
Cold plate cooling system with a spider-web channel

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Jianfeng Wang supervised the writing of this paper and provided equipment and financial support. Xiaodong Liu completed the battery experiments and the establishment and simulation of the battery model. At the same time, the simulation of the orthogonal experimental design was completed by Xiaodong Liu. Fen Liu had made a significant contributions to the conception and the design of the article. At the same time, the orthogonal design results of the paper were analyzed by Fen Liu. Yang Na was responsible for sorting out the experimental data and simulation data, and finished the original Draft. Yiqun Liu was responsible for the revision of the manuscript and the resolution of the reviewers' comments. Fuqiang Wang proposed amendments and improvements to the paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the experimental equipment.
Experimental data of the surface temperature of the battery at the discharge of 12 C.
Temperature distribution cloud diagram of the pouch battery for the discharging at 12 C.
Temperature curve of the pouch lithium-ion battery. (a) Comparison of the experiment and simulation. (b) Simulation error curve.
Schematic of the BTMS model.
Schematic of the spider-web channel structure.
Schematic of the spider-web channel.
Curves of the influences of the channel width on T max, ΔT max, and ΔP.
Curves of influences of the channel number on T max, ΔT max, and ΔP.
Curves of influences of the channel angle on T max, ΔT max, and ΔP.
Project evaluation index curve. (a) T max and ΔT max. (b) ΔP.
T max of the lithium-ion battery for discharging at 12 C.
ΔT max of the lithium-ion battery for discharging at 12 C.
Surface temperature distribution cloud map of the four schemes of battery.
Cloud diagram of the channel pressure distributions in the four schemes.
Cloud diagram of the channel velocity distributions in the four schemes.
Battery specifications.
Battery parameter tests.
Thermal properties of the medium and plate.
Levels of test factors.
Orthogonal test and results.
Range analysis results.

head.ce:title: Numerical optimization of the cooling effect of the bionic spider-web channel cold plate on a pouch lithium-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Automotive Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Weihai, Shandong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Automotive Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Weihai, Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Robotics and System, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Robotics and System

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Robotics and System, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For lithium-ion battery thermal management system for electric vehicles, the insertion of metal plates with channels on both sides of a lithium-ion battery is an effective method for a high-temperature cooling. Inspired by the nature spider-web structure, we propose a cold plate with a bionic spider-web channel inside. Based on the heat generation characteristics of the pouch lithium-ion battery at an extreme discharge rate of 12 C, we used an orthogonal experimental design to study the cooling performance of the spider web-channel structure for a lithium-ion battery. Numerical simulation results showed that, among the structural parameters of the spider-web channel, the channel width had the largest influence on the cooling performance of the cold plate, while the channel angle had the smallest influence. The increase in the channel width could not indefinitely enhance the cooling performance of the cold plate. The most suitable structure was obtained at a width of 3 mm. When the channel angle was 120°, the pouch lithium-ion battery could provide the best thermal balance.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of traditional fuel vehicles has caused an energy crisis and resulted in environmental pollution. In recent years, pure electric, hybrid, and plug-in hybrid vehicles have attracted increasing attention and have been rapidly developed. Lithium-ion batteries have become the power source for electric vehicles owing to their excellent energy densities and cycle life times. However, the temperature of lithium-ion battery pack during operation should be limited to a certain range. The battery thermal management system (BTMS) can control the temperature of the battery pack in an appropriate range of 25–40 °C. However, working conditions such as high-rate discharge will cause the temperature of the lithium-ion battery to far exceed 40 °C. Therefore, the design of an efficient lithium-ion battery thermal management system (BTMS) is extremely important for electric vehicles [1].

The lithium-ion battery pack generates a considerable heat during discharge, which results in a rapid increase in the temperature of the battery pack. If the temperature of the battery pack is too high, its performance and safety will decrease. In severe cases, it will cause spontaneous combustion or explosion of the battery, which would endanger human life. There are active and passive methods for high-temperature cooling of the battery pack. Cooling methods include air cooling [2], liquid cooling [3], and use of phase-change materials. The structure of air cooling [4] is simple and its cost is low, but the heat dissipation efficiency is low; hence, it is less frequently used nowadays. Liquid cooling includes direct and indirect contact [5,6]. In direct contact cooling, the cooling medium directly contacts the battery pack, so that the requirements for the cooling medium are very high. Regarding the indirect contact, numerous experimental and numerical studies on channel cold plates have been carried out [7–9]. The optimal cooling effect can be obtained by optimizing the cold plate structural parameters and cooling conditions [10]. When phase-change materials are used, the battery pack is placed in direct contact with the phase-change material. Phase-change materials absorb a large amount of heat upon transformation from solid to liquid, which can reduce the maximum temperature of the battery pack and improve the temperature uniformity [11,12]. Therefore, phase-change cooling is also an effective cooling method [13]. However, the phase-change cooling cannot operate over a long period and the high cost limits its use [14,15].

The cooling way of a lithium-ion battery pack with cold plates is the most effective and the most widely used approach. The optimal cooling performance and thermal balance performance of the cold plate are obtained by studying the design of the channel structure. Huang et al. [16] compared the performances of a streamlined parallel channel and right-angle parallel channel and reported that the streamlined parallel channel of plate can effectively improve the temperature uniformity of the lithium-ion battery while minimizing the pressure drop. Wang et al. [17] used orthogonal analysis to study the influence of the number of channels, different cooling structures and inlet mass flow rates of parallel channel cold plates on the temperature indicators of the battery pack. The best structure combination obtained is superior in comprehensive performance. An et al. [18] investigated mini-channel cold plates. They simplified the battery thermal management model and reduced a large number of structural parameters that need to be studied. Based on the serpentine channel, Deng et al. [19]successively analyzed the influences of the mass flow rate of the coolant, the flow direction and the number of serpentine ring on the cooling performance of the aluminum plate. Chen et al. [20] also studied the structural parameters of a cold plate serpentine channel and used the Latin HyperCubes method to generate parameter combination samples. They carried out a sensitivity analysis to determine the influence of the structural parameters on the heat dissipation performance. Xu et al. [21] added a shunt to the parallel channel cold plate to enhance the heat dissipation efficiency. They studied the influences of numerous structural parameters of the shunt on the heat dissipation performance of the aluminum plate through the orthogonal experimental method. E et al. [22] also to study the structural parameters of the cooling pipe to find the best combination model by using an orthogonal experimental method.

The bionic structure reflects the structure of the biological body in the nature and can provide an excellent heat dissipation design for humans. Yang et al. [23] designed a triangular bionic cooling structure. It was used to cool the cylindrical lithium-ion battery pack to improve heat dissipation performance. Sheng et al. [24] developed a honeycomb liquid cooling channel system. They studied the influences of the bionic honeycomb liquid cooling structure on the cooling of cylindrical battery through numerical and experimental methods. The results showed that the honeycomb cooling system can well control the maximum discharge temperature of the cylindrical battery pack. Yang et al. [25] combined honeycomb liquid cooling and phase-change cooling to study the performance of the composite cooling structure on the heat dissipation of the battery pack. Dong et al. [26] proposed a double helix liquid cooling structure suitable for the cylindrical lithium-ion battery. The effect of coolant mass flow, spiral groove spacing and flow diameter on cooling performance was studied by numerical simulation.

The research on liquid cooling of the pouch lithium-ion battery mostly used serpentine channel cold plate or parallel channel cold plate. Bionic-structure radiators were mostly used in electronic chips or aerospace applications. There are few studies on the heat dissipation of the lithium-ion battery. In this study, a cold plate with a bionic spider-web channel is proposed for the first time, which draws on the excellent heat dissipation structure of natural spider webs. Then an orthogonal experimental design is used to study the effects of the parameters of the spider-web channel including the channel width (l), channel number (n), and channel angle (α) on the cooling performance and thermal balance performance of the cold plate. The developed lithium-ion battery heat generation model can be used to accurately simulate the temperature response of an extreme discharge of 12 C and provides a model reference for the establishment of a pouch lithium-ion battery cooling system. The aim of this paper is to propose a new cold plate channel structure and find the best combination of different structural parameters of the spider web channel. This paper can enrich the design of BTMS.

We used a LiCoO2 pouch battery with a capacity of 7 Ah. The specifications of the battery are shown in Table 1 . To evaluate the temperature increase of the LiCoO2 battery at 25 °C, a battery tester, constant-temperature test chamber, and data acquisition instrument were used. Five temperature sensors were arranged on the surface of the LiCoO2 battery to measure the temperatures of different areas, as shown in Fig. 1 .

This paper evaluated the temperature measurement results of different thermocouples through uncertainty analysis. The uncertainty of the thermocouple measurement results was calculated within the range of ±0.58 K. Therefore, the temperature measurement result of the lithium-ion battery was credible [27,28].

The pouch lithium-ion battery was placed in the constant-temperature test chamber, while the battery tester controled the lithium-ion battery for constant current discharge. Then the temperature response of the lithium-ion battery at a discharge rate of 12C was measured with the data logger and thermocouples.

Fig. 2 shows the temperature data for the LiCoO2 battery discharged at 12 C. The area with the highest temperature on the surface of the cell was close to the positive-electrode tab, which reached 64.16 °C. The edge area had the lowest battery cell temperature of 62.44 °C, which is considerably beyond the suitable operation temperature range of the battery. Therefore, the lithium-ion battery cooling system is very important.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat generation model of the pouch lithium-ion battery
ce:section.ce:para:
The lithium-ion battery generates a large amount of heat upon discharging owing to the internal electro-chemical reactions. In general, to simplify the calculation, the heat is divided into four parts, Ohmic heat Q
j , reaction heat Q
r ,polarization heat Q
p , and side-reaction heat Q
s . Q s generally occurs in heat abuse such as over-charging and over-discharging. It is hardly generated during normal operation, and thus it can be ignored [21].
(1)
Q =
Q j
+
Q p
+
Q r
+
Q s

Based on the three different types of heat, Bernardi et al. proposed a widely used heat generation rate model in 1985 to roughly simulate the heat generation rate of a battery, where [29].
(2)
q =
I V
(
I R ± T
∂
U
O C V
∂ T
)
where I represents the discharge current (A), V represents the volume of the battery (m3), R represents the internal resistance of the battery (Ω), including the Ohmic internal resistance and polarization internal resistance,
∂
U
O C V
/ ∂ T
represents the entropy coefficient (V/K), and T represents the temperature of the battery (K).

The Ohmic internal resistance and polarization internal resistance are affected by the battery SOC and temperature, which can be measured by the HPPC (hybrid pulse power) test [30]. In addition, the entropy coefficients of different SOCs can be determined by measuring the open-circuit voltage changes of the lithium-ion battery at different temperatures [30]. With the above equipment, the HPPC (hybrid pulse power) test and entropy coefficient test (presented in Table 2 ) were carried out. By fitting the battery's internal resistance and entropy coefficient, the 12C heat production rate formula of the battery was obtained [24].
(3)
q =
− 8.798
e
− 10
×
t 5
− 1.606
e
− 10
×
t 4
T + 1.138
e
− 6
×
t 4
− 3.567
e
− 8
×
t 3
T 2
+ 2.037
e
− 5
×
t 3
T
− 3.777
e
− 3
×
t 3
+
6.212
e
− 5
×
t 2
T 2
−
0.03701 ×
t 2
T + 5.737 ×
t 2
−
0.01763 × t
T 2
+
10.77 t T
−
1654 t + 50.43
T 2
− 33400 T +
5.565
e 6
+
19822 T × sin
(
0.00386 × t +
0.1403
)
+ 40.2 T × sin
(
0.02558 × t +
3.73
)
+
19844 T × sin
(
0.003889 × t +
3.274
)
where q represents the volumetric heat production rate of the battery (W/m3), t represents the time (s), and T represents the average temperature of the battery (K).

In this study, the STAR-CCM + software was used to simulate the temperature distribution cloud diagram of the lithium-ion battery at a discharge rate of 12 C, in which the surface convective heat transfer coefficient was set to 7.5 W m-2 K-1. The surface and internal temperature distribution cloud map of the pouch battery was shown in Fig. 3 . The temperature at the center point of the battery surface was monitored. The simulation data were then compared to the experimental data, as shown in Fig. 4 . The model is statistically compared with the experiment. The mean square error (MAE) between the simulated and experimental data is 0.85 °C, the root mean square error (RMSE) is 0.88 °C, and the percentage root mean square error (PRMSE) is 2.04% [31,32]. Therefore, the thermal model in this study has a high accuracy and can provide model references for subsequent battery cooling simulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cold plate cooling system with a spider-web channel

ce:section.ce:para: A LiCoO2 pouch lithium-ion battery and aluminum plate were used. Two aluminum plates with a spider-web channel were clamped on both sides of the pouch battery. The cooling system was used only to cool the cell of the pouch battery. The three-dimensional model is shown in Fig. 5 . The medium flows through the channel of the aluminum plate and performs convective heat exchange with the aluminum plate. The aluminum plate and lithium-ion battery are involved in heat transfer to cool the battery. The cooling medium was a (50:50) water–ethylene glycol mixture with a freezing point of −35 °C. The thermophysical parameters of the aluminum plate and water–glycol mixture (50:50) are listed in Table 3 . It was assumed that the various parameters of the aluminum plate and medium are isotropic and do not change with temperature.
The thicknesses of the aluminum plate and rectangular-cross-section channel were set to 4 and 2 mm, respectively, while the height and width of the aluminum plate were 140 and 45 mm, respectively.

During the cooling simulation process, the lithium-ion battery was discharged at a volumetric heat generation rate of 12 C. The thermal resistance between the battery and aluminum plate was ignored owing to the relatively large surface area. The temperature of the battery, aluminum plate, and external environment was set to 25 °C. The inlet temperature of the cooling medium was set to 25 °C, while the mass flow rate was 0.5 g s−1. The sides of the battery were set to natural convection with the outside, while the convection heat transfer coefficient was 7.5 W K−1 m−2.

The energy-saving equation of the lithium-ion battery is [19].
(4)
ρ b
c p b
∂
T b
∂ t
= ∇ ⋅
(
k b
∇
T b
)
+ q
where
q
is the heat generation of the battery (W·m−3),
ρ b
is the density of the battery (kg·m−3),
c p b
is the thermal capacity of the battery (J·kg−1 K−1), and
k b
is the thermal conductivity of the battery (W·m−1 K−1).

The mass conservation, momentum conservation, and energy conservation equations of the cooling and heating media are [19].
(5)
∂
ρ m
∂ t
+ ∇ ⋅
(
ρ m
v
)
= 0
(6)
∂
ρ m
∂ t
v + ∇ ⋅
(
ρ m
v
)
v = − ∇ p + μ
∇ 2
v
(7)
ρ m
c p m
∂ T
∂ t
+ ∇ ⋅
(
ρ m
c p m
v T
)
= ∇ ⋅
(
k l
∇ T
)
where
ρ m
indicates the density of the medium (kg·m−3),
v
is the flow rate of the medium (m·s−1),
μ
indicates the dynamic viscosity of the medium (Pa·s),
c p m
is the heat capacity of the medium (J·kg−1 K−1), and
k l
is the thermal conductivity of the medium (W·m−1 K−1).

The energy conservation equation of the aluminum plate is [19].
(8)
ρ P
c p p
∂ T
∂ t
= ∇ ·
(
k p
∇ T
)
where
ρ p
is the density of the aluminum plate (kg·m−3),
c p p
is the heat capacity of the aluminum plate (J·kg−1 K−1), and
k p
is the thermal conductivity of the aluminum plate (W·m−1 K−1).

The pressure drop of the interior channel is calculated by [18].
(9)
Δ P =
v 2
L
ρ m
f
2 D
where
v
is the flow rate of the medium (m·s−1),
L
is the length of the channel along the flowing direction (m),
ρ m
is the density of the medium (kg·m−3),
D
is the hydraulic diameter of the channel (m), and
f
is the friction factor.

Numerous factors affect the heat dissipation effect of the cold plate of the spider-web channel, such as the channel thickness, channel angle, channel number, and channel width. If multiple factors are considered and all experiments with multiple factors are carried out, the number of experiments will be large. Therefore, we chose the orthogonal test method to optimize the design of the spider-web channel structure.

The orthogonal test design can be used to study multiple factors and multiple levels. It can selects average discrete and representative tests from a large number of tests to find the best structure combination. So it can simplify the number of tests to reduce the work cost and workload. For example, for a three-factor four-level test, the total number of tests is 64, which is too large. The orthogonal experiment design can select only 16 of them for the experiment, which largely reduces the number of tests.

In this section, we use the L 16 (43) orthogonal table to carry out a three-factor four-level test. The maximum temperature (T max) and maximum temperature difference (ΔT max) of the cell were used as the evaluation indices of the cooling effect. The pressure drop (ΔP) of the aluminum plate channel was also used as a reference.

The three structural parameters of the spider-web channel are the channel thickness (l), channel angle (α), and channel number (n), as shown in Fig. 6 . Other dimensions are fixed. The orthogonal table is shown in Table 4 . In the 16 schemes, computational fluid dynamics (CFD) numerical simulation methods were used. The CFD models were developed and postprocessed with the STAR-CCM + software. The scheme of 16 types of spider-web-structure channels is shown in Fig. 7 . The results of the orthogonal experiment design are shown in Table 5 .

In the orthogonal test, the range (R) of each factor can directly reflect the degree of influence of different factors on the evaluation index. A larger range (R) implies a larger degree of influence. So We can used the range (R) of the orthogonal test results to evaluate the influences of the factors on the heat dissipation performance and power loss of the cold plate. The optimal combination of the spider-web channel structure parameters could be obtained.

Therefore, the orthogonal test results can be analyzed by calculating the range (R) by Equation (10), where k i is the average of the sum of the levels of each factor,
(10)
R = max
{
k 1
,
k 2
,
k 3
,
k 4
}
− min
{
k 1
,
k 2
,
k 3
,
k 4
}

The range calculation results are shown in Table 6 . For the maximum temperature (T max) of the cell area, the number of channels (n) had the largest R, followed by that of the channel width (l), while the channel angle (α) has the smallest R according to the range calculation results. Therefore, the number of channels (n) has the largest influence on the maximum temperature; the influence degrees of the factors can be ordered as n > l > α.

In the same manner, for the maximum temperature difference (ΔT max) of the cell, the range of the channel width (l) is the largest and thus it has the largest impact, followed by the channel angle (α), while that of the number of channels (n) is smallest. For the pressure drop (ΔP) of the cold plate, the order is l > n > α.
In summary, the channel width (l) has the largest impact on the cooling performance and power loss of the cold plate, while the channel angle (α) has the smallest impact.

The influences of different levels of a single factor on the cooling of the lithium-ion battery were analyzed, as shown in Figs. 8–10
. As the channel width (l) increased, the maximum temperature (T max) and maximum temperature difference (ΔT max) of the lithium-ion battery cell decreased, as the increase in channel width increases the convection heat exchange area between the fluid and cold plate, thereby increasing the cooling performance and thermal balance performance. The pressure drop (ΔP) of the cold plate also exhibited a decreasing trend. Simultaneously, the increase in the channel width increases the hydraulic diameter, which decreased ΔP between the inlet and outlet of the cold plate. However, with the increase in the.

channel width, the decrease trends of T max and ΔT max gradually decreased, which indicates that the increase in the width cannot indefinitely increase the cooling performance. Therefore, when l = 3 mm, the cold plate had the highest cooling performance.

In addition, the increase in the number of shunt channels (n) also increased the heat convection exchange area and reduced the maximum temperature (T max) of the lithium-ion battery. The increase in the number of shunt channels reduced the fluid flow rate of each channel, resulting in a decrease in ΔP of the cold plate. When the number of channels is n < 9, ΔT max of the cell hardly changed. When n > 9, ΔT max of the cell was significantly reduced. Although the increase in the number of channels led to an increase in the convective heat transfer area, it also reduced the flow velocity of the shunt channels. When n < 9, the thermal balance performance of the cold plate hardly changed, while the thermal balance performance of the cold plate gradually increased when n > 9. Therefore, when n = 11, the cold plate exhibited the highest cooling performance while the power loss was relatively small.

As the angle of the shunt channel (α) increased, T max of the cell and ΔP of the cold plate exhibited decreasing trends, while ΔT max of the cell exhibited an increasing trend. When α increases from 120° to 140°, T max of the cell did not largely change, but ΔT max of the cell significantly increased, so that the angle of the shunt channel cannot be reduced indefinitely to reduce the maximum temperature of the battery. Therefore, when α = 120°, the heat balance performance of the cold plate can be fully considered.

In summary, the optimal structure of the spider-web channel is defined by l 4 = 3 mm, n 4 = 11, and α 3 = 120°, which provided the highest cooling performance and heat balance performance of the cold plate and reduced its loss.

In this section, we simulate the relatively optimal model to obtain the maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference of the pouch lithium-ion battery and pressure drop of the cold plate. Fig. 11 shows T max and ΔT max of the battery for the 16 schemes and optimized scheme. The cold plate of the optimal model enabled T max of the lithium-ion battery to reach 37.892 °C, so that the highest cooling performance can be obtained. At this time, the heat balance performance of the cold plate was also excellent. ΔT max is 8.864 °C, almost equal to that for scheme 16. ΔP of the cold-plate channel was also smaller than those of the other 16 schemes (27.06 Pa).
The numerical simulation results showed that the relatively optimal model can provide the highest cooling performance and heat balance performance with a lower loss.

To elucidate the performance of the relatively optimal model, schemes 12, 15, and 16 were selected for comparison to the relatively optimal scheme. The plans 12 and 17 differ only in channel width, the plans 15 and 17 differ in the number of channels, and the plans 16 and 17 differ in the channel angle. In this section, we compared the maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference of the four schemes of the lithium-ion battery during the discharge process, as shown in Figs. 12 and 13
. Fig. 12 shows that T max of the relatively optimal solution (17) was always lower than those of the other three solutions when the battery was discharged at a large rate of 12 C. In the rapid-temperature-increase stage at the end of the discharge, the cooling performance of scheme 17 was prominent. According to the above analysis, the number of channels (n) had the largest impact on T max, while the channel angle (α) had the smallest impact. Therefore, scheme 15 with the smallest number of channels among the four schemes exhibited the largest temperature. The channel angle of scheme 16 is smaller than that of scheme 12, while the channel width is larger than that of scheme 12, so that the cooling performance was better than that of plan 12. Regarding ΔT max of the lithium-ion battery, the thermal balance performance of scheme 17 was second-highest, only slightly lower than that of scheme 16.

According to the temperature distribution cloud chart (see Fig. 14 ), the temperature of the terminal area of the battery surface of the optimal model was significantly lower than those of the other solutions. The temperature distributions of the solutions 12 and 15 were almost the same. In the front area of the battery surface, the program 15 had the highest temperature, while those of schemes 12 and 16 were almost the same; that of the optimal program was significantly lower than those of the other three programs. Therefore, the cooling performance of the relatively optimal model was significantly better than those of the other solutions.

In addition, in this section, we compare the flow channel pressure and velocity distribution cloud maps of the four schemes, as shown in Figs. 15 and 16
. The flow rates of the channel fluid in schemes 16 and 17 were significantly lower than those in schemes 12 and 15. ΔP of the 16 and 17 schemes were almost equal. The flow velocities in the channel were also almost equal. The channel angle had the smallest influence on ΔP.

We proposed a new type of cold plate with a spider-web channel and studied its cooling performance and thermal balance performance for a pouch lithium-ion battery. We obtained the volume heat production rate of the pouch lithium-ion battery at 12 C through the HPPC hybrid pulse power test and entropy coefficient measurement. The battery heat production model obtained by the numerical simulation accurately simulated the temperature distribution of the battery at a discharge rate of 12 C. The mean square error (MAE) between the simulated and experimental data is 0.85 °C, the root mean square error (RMSE) is 0.88 °C, and the percentage root mean square error (PRMSE) is only 2.04%. This provided an accuratemodel reference for the design of the cold plate structure.

The cold plate cooling system was then established. An orthogonal test table with three factors and four levels was used to find the best combination of different structural parameters of the spider-web channel. The simulation results showed the channel width (l) had the largest influence on the performance of the cold plate, the number of channels (n) was the second, while the channel angle (α) had the smallest influence. The best combination obtained by orthogonal experiment included l 4 = 3 mm, n 4 = 11, and α 3 = 120°, at which the pouch lithium-ion battery exhibited the lowest temperature (37.892 °C) and the lowest temperature difference (8.864 °C) when cold plate had the lower pump loss (27.06 Pa). The cold plate of the relatively optimal model could effectively reduce the temperature of the battery surface and improve the temperature uniformity. This research provided a new form of cold plate for the liquid cooling battery pack.

Jianfeng Wang supervised the writing of this paper and provided equipment and financial support. Xiaodong Liu completed the battery experiments and the establishment and simulation of the battery model. At the same time, the simulation of the orthogonal experimental design was completed by Xiaodong Liu. Fen Liu had made a significant contributions to the conception and the design of the article. At the same time, the orthogonal design results of the paper were analyzed by Fen Liu. Yang Na was responsible for sorting out the experimental data and simulation data, and finished the original Draft. Yiqun Liu was responsible for the revision of the manuscript and the resolution of the reviewers' comments. Fuqiang Wang proposed amendments and improvements to the paper.
The authors mentioned in the manuscript all contributed to this paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported in part by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2020ME129), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51905121).
